titleStmt.title: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark), to the Joint Chiefs of Staff

div.note: FE files, lot 55 D 128, tab 34: Telegram

head.hi: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark), to the Joint Chiefs of Staff

list.item: Since General Ridgway notified you in para 3 of referenced message of his decision to hold all civilian internees until the plan for their release could be included in the overall plan for the release of all internees, both military and civilian, there have been several developments which indicate that those CIs who have been screened and have indicated they do not wish to return to Communists control, should be released from UNC custody as soon as appropriate arrangements are made with ROK.

In arriving at the above decision, I have carefully examined all aspects of this problem and have discussed it personally with Van Fleet and Harrison, both of whom recommend release. The primary deterrent to releasing the CIs to date has been the adverse effect upon the armistice negotiations and a possible retaliation against UN POWs. However, this deterrent is not considered valid at this time. Harrison submitted to me his analysis of the question, pertinent parts of which are quoted below:
“The civilian internees are not POWs in fact, having been reclassified as civilians and so reported to the ICRC. They therefore are, or should be, outside of the POW issue. In item 4 staff discussions the Communists tacitly accepted this view when the [they] proposed POW exchange based on 18 December lists. The release of the civilian internees I consider a matter of justice consistent with our position on the entire POW issue. If we do not release them it can be inferred that our proclaimed final stand is not actually final and that we are so afraid of not achieving an armistice that we are prepared to sacrifice our widely publicized humanitarian principles in the interests of expediency. Release of the civilian internees would tend to prove that our stand is final and that we do not fear the consequences of the act.
The objection has been advanced that their release may influence the enemy to break off negotiations or to delay an armistice indefinitely. I consider this risk negligible. Although current enemy statements indicate that they include these persons among those they want returned to them, their earlier statements in staff discussions indicate their willingness to ignore them. I consider it very unlikely that a major decision would be determined by an issue that the enemy has already discounted.
I also consider it a negligible risk that release of civilian internees might cause enemy to retaliate by holding UN POWs now in their hands. The only way we can expect the return of our men is through an armistice. If the enemy wants an armistice sufficiently to agree to our package proposal there will be no point in his not exchanging the 12,000 UN prisoners for the 70,000 we hold, as specified in that proposal. We can exercise some degree of control by regulating the rate of exchange of the prisoners on a proportionate basis. For the enemy not to return the UN prisoners, or to threaten not to return them in order to influence negotiations would be a complete repudiation of all that he has said in the conferences and to the world about repatriation. In my opinion the probability of the return of our men in enemy hands is approximately equal to the probability of achieving an armistice.
While we cannot accurately foresee the consequences of releasing the civilian internees, it would appear reasonably certain that the enemy will use it as fuel for his propaganda and, if he desires, to justify delay or prevention of an armistice. However, he already has ample propaganda material and can manufacture more at will. Fear of what the enemy might say should not, in my opinion, deter action otherwise indicated. I believe that in case of doubt the bolder course should be followed.

The question of the continued detention of the CIs has been a constant source of irritation to the ROK govt and the ROK people. In this connection, Pres Rhee has repeatedly made strong representations to CGArEight and AmEmbassy (Muccio) for the release of CIs. Delay in their release will unquestionably result in unrest and further incidents such as that which took place in compound nbr 14 Yongchon, reported to you in para 3B, CX 69308 and para 3 CX 69622.Neither of these telegrams, dated May 30 and June 4, is printed (FE files, lot 55 D 128, tabs 151 and 20, respectively). We anticipate increased activity by infiltrated Communist agents attempting to further this unrest. It is also believed that the release of the CIs can be accomplished without materially affecting the present crisis within the ROK govt.

In addition to the advantages outlined above, the release of approximately 27,000 CIs would materially reduce the logistic burden of the military and free vitally needed overhead personnel for other duties.
The timing and general plan for the release of the CIs will be furnished you before actual release begins.

Although I consider the decision to release CIs within my province, in view of the effect such action might have on the armistice negotiations, request your views soonest.The Joint Chiefs of Staff approved this proposal in principle on June 10, 1952 in JCS 910811 to Clark (FE files, lot 55 D 128, tab 82).
